locked_facts: Average KE per molecule: KE = (3/2)k_BT (k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K)
Temperature is a measure of average translational kinetic energy of molecules
At same T, lighter molecules move faster: v_rms ∝ 1/√m
Degrees of freedom: monatomic (3), diatomic (5), polyatomic (6-7)
Equipartition of energy: each degree of freedom gets ½k_BT per molecule
truth_statements: Temperature is fundamentally a measure of the average translational kinetic energy of the molecules of a substance
At the same temperature lighter gas molecules move faster than heavier ones
The pressure exerted by a gas is due to the momentum transfer during molecular collisions with the walls
The equipartition theorem assigns ½k_BT energy to each degree of freedom per molecule
Absolute zero (0 K) is the temperature at which molecular motion would theoretically cease — it can never be reached

aha_moment.what_to_show: A mixture of heavy (blue, slow) and light (red, fast) molecules at the same temperature. Both have the SAME average KE = (3/2)k_BT, but the light molecules zoom around much faster. Temperature is about energy, not speed.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Heavy slow molecules and light fast molecules side by side with equal KE bars but very different speed bars
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students confuse temperature with speed — seeing equal KE with different speeds clarifies that temperature measures energy, not velocity

simulation_states.label: Molecules in motion
simulation_states.description: Gas molecules bouncing around in a box. Random directions and speeds. Collisions with walls produce pressure. Temperature represented by colour intensity.
simulation_states.emphasis: Gas pressure comes from trillions of molecular collisions per second

simulation_states.label: Temperature = average KE
simulation_states.description: KE measured for many molecules. Distribution shown (Maxwell-Boltzmann). Average KE = (3/2)k_BT. Higher T → higher average KE → faster molecules.
simulation_states.emphasis: Temperature IS average kinetic energy — nothing more, nothing less

simulation_states.label: Light vs heavy molecules
simulation_states.description: H₂ and O₂ at same temperature. Same average KE. But H₂ (light) moves 4× faster than O₂ (heavy). v_rms = √(3k_BT/m).
simulation_states.emphasis: Same temperature = same KE but NOT same speed. Lighter = faster.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts temperature and molecular mass. RMS speed, KE, and pressure update. Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution shifts. Mix two gases to see different speeds at same T.
simulation_states.emphasis: Raise temperature — molecules move faster. Change mass — speed changes but KE stays same at same T.

common_misconceptions.belief: All molecules at a given temperature have the same speed
common_misconceptions.correction: Molecules have a DISTRIBUTION of speeds (Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution). Temperature determines the average KE, but individual molecules have widely varying speeds — some fast, some slow.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show speed distribution histogram — wide spread around the average

common_misconceptions.belief: Temperature measures how hot something feels
common_misconceptions.correction: Temperature is the average translational kinetic energy of molecules: KE = (3/2)k_BT. How 'hot' something feels depends on thermal conductivity and heat capacity, not just temperature. Metal and wood at the same T feel different.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show molecular KE directly proportional to T with numerical values
